FULL CONTENT:

![Advertisement](//pubads.g.doubleclick.net/gampad/ad?iu=/270604982/springer_open/comparativemigrationstudies/articles&sz=728x90,970x90&pos=LB1&doi=10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2&type=article&pmc=X24000,X22000,W38000&)
![SpringerOpen](/static/images/springeropen/logo-springer-open-d04c3ea16c.svg)

# Can internet search data predict human migration intentions?

[*Comparative Migration Studies*](/)
**volume 13**, Article number: 28 (2025)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

753 Accesses

5 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2/metrics)

## Abstract

Internet search data may reveal people’s intentions to migrate, as aspiring migrants tend to use online search engines to explore migration opportunities. However, unlike official migration statistics, search data may only reflect the behavior of a self-selected subset of the population, raising concerns about its generalizability. This article integrates traditional survey data - Gallup World Poll (GWP) - with Google Trends, search engine market share, and internet adoption rate to examine the extent to which search trends of migration-related topics can serve as proxies for migration intentions. The results reveal that, on a global scale, passport-related search queries strongly correlate with individuals’ intentions to migrate. However, at the country level, particularly in the global south, migration intentions are more accurately predicted by the adoption rates of Google search rather than search topics per se. These findings underscore the importance of detecting and correcting user selection biases when leveraging digital trace data for migration research, ensuring robust and representative insights into the spatial-temporal patterns of human mobility.

## Introduction

With the growing adoption of large-scale organic data and machine learning techniques, computational migration research has made significant progress in recent years (Drouhot et al., [2022](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR26 "Drouhot, L. G., Deutschmann, E., Zuccotti, C. V., & Zagheni, E. (2022). Computational approaches to migration and integration research: Promises and challenges. Journal of Ethnic and Migration Studies, 49, 389–407. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1080/1369183x.2022.2100542
                  
                ") ). Digital trace data such as geolocated social media activities, detailed mobile phone records, and online search trends offer new insights into migration behavior, filling gaps in traditional statistics and enabling more timely policy responses. However, these data sources are not without limitations, particularly regarding sample selectivity (Tjaden, [2021](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR45 "Tjaden, J. (2021). Measuring migration 2.0: A review of digital data sources. Comparative Migration Studies")). Unlike official migration data, digital trace data are drawn from non-random populations, with user bases that vary across platforms, countries, and time periods (Hargittai, [2015](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR30 "Hargittai, E. (2015). Is bigger always better?? Potential biases of big data derived from social network sites. The ANNALS of the American Academy of Political and Social Science, 659, 63–76. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1177/0002716215570866
                  
                "), [2018](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR29 "Hargittai, E. (2018). Potential biases in big data: Omitted voices on social media. Social Science Computer Review, 10")). This raises a critical question: To what extent can findings from digital trace data be generalized beyond their user population?

Recent studies have attempted to mitigate sampling biases by integrating digital trace data with surveys, censuses, and population registers (Rampazzo, [2021](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR40 "Rampazzo, F. (2021). B. J. A Framework for Estimating Migrant Stocks Using Digital Traces and Survey Data: An Application in the United Kingdom. Demography"), [2024](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR41 "Rampazzo, F. B. (2024). Assessing timely migration trends through digital traces: A case study of the UK before brexit. International Migration Review"); Yildiz, [2024](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR49 "Yildiz, D. W. (2024). Integrating traditional and social media data to predict bilateral migrant stocks in the European union. International Migration Review")). However, many challenges remain. Notably, data availability is inconsistent across countries, platform usage varies over time and space, algorithms identifying user location history are not transparent, and the usage rates of different platforms (e.g., Google, Facebook) in different settings are mostly unknown.

Given the heterogeneity of digital trace data, developing a general approach to correcting for sample selection biases is very challenging. This article focuses specifically on Google Trends (GT) which collates online search behavior at the country-level over time, offering insights into people’s interests and possible migration intentions (Varian, [2014](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR47 "Varian, H. R. (2014). Big data: New tricks for econometrics. Journal of Economic Perspectives, 28, 3–28")). Unlike other digital trace data, search queries may capture ex-ante intention to migrate, as aspiring migrants often use search engines to explore migration opportunities, challenges, and resources (Maitland & Xu, [2015](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR32 "Maitland, C., & Xu, Y. (2015). A social informatics analysis of refugee mobile phone use. A case study of Za’atari Syrian refugee camp")).

While GT has been used to measure migration intentions and predict mobility patterns (Böhme et al., [2020](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR7 "Böhme, M. H., Gröger, A., & Stöhr, T. (2020). Searching for a better life: Predicting international migration with online search keywords. Journal of Development Economics, 142, 102347"); Wanner, [2020](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR48 "Wanner, P. (2020). How well can we estimate immigration trends using Google data? Quality & Quantity, 55, 1181–1202. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s11135-020-01047-w
                  
                "); Avramescu & Wiśniowski, [2021](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR3 "Avramescu, A., & Wiśniowski, A. (2021). Now-casting Romanian migration into the united Kingdom by using Google search engine data. Demographic Research, 45, 1219–1254. 
                  https://doi.org/10.4054/demres.2021.45.40
                  
                "); Carammia et al., [2022](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR12 "Carammia, M., Iacus, S. M., & Wilkin, T. (2022). Forecasting asylum-related migration flows with machine learning and data at scale. Scientific Reports, 12, 1–16")), its reliability is debatable (Qi & Bircan, [2023b](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR38 "Qi, H., & Bircan, T. (2023b). Can Google Trends predict asylum-seekers’ destination choices? EPJ Data Science, 12. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1140/epjds/s13688-023-00419-0
                  
                ")). To assess GT’s reliability, this article introduces a validation model which (i) examines whether GT search topics can directly measure the average person’s migration intentions in a given country; and (ii) constructs latent measures of migration intentions adjusted for sampling biases.

Using Gallup World Poll (GWP) survey data, coupled with GT search topics, search engine market share, and internet adoption rates at the country level, we show that GT alone does not provide an unbiased measure of migration intentions. Globally, passport-related searches correlate with migration intentions, but in many Global South countries, the adoption rates of Google search (the proportion of population using the internet multiplied by the market share of the Google search engine) are more predictive of migration intentions, than search topics. Thus, this paper cautions researchers against relying on digital trace data alone to assess migration intentions and proposes potential mechanisms for adjusting for the inherent sampling biases.

The paper proceeds as follows. First, we discuss GT’s potential contributions and biases. We then outline our methodology and present validation results. We conclude with implications for future research and migration policymaking.

## Modeling human migration: recent advances and methodological challenges

The gravity model of human migration has been fundamental to many quantitative analyses of why and how people move. Serving as the cornerstone for economists to study how migration behavior is shaped by economic incentives (Bertoli & Moraga, [2013](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR5 "Bertoli, S., & Moraga, J. F. H. (2013). Multilateral resistance to migration. Journal of Development Economics, 102, 79–100. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jdeveco.2012.12.001
                  
                "); Beine et al., [2015](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR4 "Beine, M., Bertoli, S., & Moraga, J. F. H. (2015). March). A practitioners’ guide to gravity models of international migration. The World Economy, 39, 496–512. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1111/twec.12265
                  
                "); Grogger & Hanson, [2011](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR28 "Grogger, J., & Hanson, G. H. (2011). Income maximization and the selection and sorting of international migrants. Journal of Development Economics, 95, 42–57. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jdeveco.2010.06.003
                  
                "); Clark et al., [2007](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR17 "Clark, X., Hatton, T. J., & Williamson, J. G. (2007). Explaining U.S. Immigration, 1971–1998. The Review of Economics and Statistics, 89, 359–373. Retrieved from 
                  http://www.jstor.org/stable/40043066
                  
                "); Pedersen et al., [2008](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR36 "Pedersen, P. J., Pytlikova, M., & Smith, N. (2008). Selection and network effects—Migration flows into OECD countries 1990–2000. European Economic Review, 52, 1160–1186. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.euroecorev.2007.12.002
                  
                "); Mayda, [2010](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR35 "Mayda, A. M. (2010). International migration: a panel data analysis of the determinants of bilateral flows. Journal of Population Economics, 23, 1249–1274. Retrieved from 
                  http://www.jstor.org/stable/40925859
                  
                ")), the gravity model has also been widely used to infer migration responses to climate and socio-political changes (Abel et al., [2019](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR1 "Abel, G. J., Brottrager, M., Cuaresma, J. C., & Muttarak, R. (2019). Climate, conflict and forced migration. Global Environmental Change, 54, 239–249. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2018.12.003
                  
                "); Black et al., [2011](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR6 "Black, R., Bennett, S. R., Thomas, S. M., & Beddington, J. R. (2011). Migration as adaptation. Nature, 478, 447–449. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1038/478477a
                  
                "); Cattaneo et al., [2019](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR16 "Cattaneo, C., Beine, M., Fröhlich, C. J., Kniveton, D., Martinez-Zarzoso, I., Mastrorillo, M., & Schraven, B. (2019). Human migration in the era of climate change. Review of Environmental Economics and Policy, 13, 189–206. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1093/reep/rez008
                  
                "); Clement et al., [2021](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR18 "Clement, V., Rigaud, K., De Sherbinin, A., Jones, B., Adamo, S., Schewe, J., & Shabahat, E. (2021). Groundswell part 2: Acting on internal climate migration. World Bank. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1596/36248
                  
                "); Rigaud et al., [2018](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR42 "Rigaud, K., De Sherbinin, A., Jones, B., Casals Fernandez, A., & Adamo, S. (2018). Groundswell: Preparing for internal climate migration. World Bank. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1596/36447
                  
                ")).

However, the gravity model has been criticized for its unrealistic assumption that all migrants are forward-looking utility maximizers who move voluntarily and spontaneously for better opportunities and livelihoods elsewhere. This assumption has been contested by the notion of involuntary immobility; most people do not have the capability to migrate as they wish to take advantage of cross-country differences in income and economic opportunities (Carling, [2002](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR13 "Carling, J. (2002). Migration in the age of involuntary immobility: Theoretical reflections and cape Verdean experiences. Journal of Ethnic and Migration Studies, 28, 5–42"); Carling & Schewel, [2018](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR15 "Carling, J., & Schewel, K. (2018). Revisiting aspiration and ability in international migration. Journal of Ethnic and Migration Studies, 44, 945–963"); Collins, [2018](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR19 "Collins, F. L. (2018). Desire as a theory for migration studies: Temporality, assemblage and becoming in the narratives of migrants. Journal of Ethnic and Migration Studies, 44, 964–980"); De Haas, [2021](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR24 "De Haas, H. (2021). A Theory of migration: The aspirations-capabilities framework. Comparative Migration Studies, 9, 1–35"); Schewel, [2020](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR43 "Schewel, K. (2020). Understanding immobility: Moving beyond the mobility bias in migration studies. International Migration Review, 54, 328–355")). Other migration scholarship has recognized that, even under optimal capability to migrate, migration actions are also shaped and influenced by families and households, social norms and taboos, and community influences (Andersson, [2014](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR2 "Andersson, R. (2014). Time and the migrant other: European border controls and the Temporal economics of illegality. American Anthropologist, 795–809"); Carling, [2024](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR14 "Carling, J. (2024). Why do people migrate?? Fresh takes on the foundational question of migrate?ion studies. International Migration Review, 1757–1791"); De Haas, [2010](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR23 "De Haas, H. (2010). Migration and development: A theoretical perspective. International Migration Review, 227–264"); Massey, [1993](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR33 "Massey, D. S. (1993). Theories of international migration: A review and appraisal. Population and Development Review, 431–466"); Massey et al., [1999](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR34 "Massey, D. S., Arango, J., Hugo, G., Kouaouci, A., & Pellegrino, A. (1999). Worlds in motion: Understanding international migration at the end of the millennium: Understanding international migration at the end of the millennium. Clarendon")). Moreover, motivations for migration are frequently multidimensional and difficult to measure given poor quality and availability of conventional data (Donato et al., [2025](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR25 "Donato, K., Jacobs, E., Singh, L., Arab, A., & Wycoff, N. (2025). Using organic data in migration research. International Migration Review, 5–36"); Crawley & Skleparis, [2018](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR21 "Crawley, H., & Skleparis, D. (2018). Refugees, migrants, neither, both: Categorical fetishism and the politics of bounding in Europe’s ‘migration crisis’. Journal of Ethnic and Migration Studies, 48–64")).

In recent years, a new class of gravity models emerged in an attempt to reconcile these competing theoretical perspectives. Specifically, in addition to conventional migration drivers (e.g., economic, climate, and/or political), the models also consider people’s intentions to migrate as well as the possibility of realizing their intended mobility (Böhme et al., [2020](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR7 "Böhme, M. H., Gröger, A., & Stöhr, T. (2020). Searching for a better life: Predicting international migration with online search keywords. Journal of Development Economics, 142, 102347"); Wanner, [2020](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR48 "Wanner, P. (2020). How well can we estimate immigration trends using Google data? Quality & Quantity, 55, 1181–1202. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s11135-020-01047-w
                  
                "); Avramescu & Wiśniowski, [2021](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR3 "Avramescu, A., & Wiśniowski, A. (2021). Now-casting Romanian migration into the united Kingdom by using Google search engine data. Demographic Research, 45, 1219–1254. 
                  https://doi.org/10.4054/demres.2021.45.40
                  
                "); Carammia et al., [2022](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR12 "Carammia, M., Iacus, S. M., & Wilkin, T. (2022). Forecasting asylum-related migration flows with machine learning and data at scale. Scientific Reports, 12, 1–16")). Hereafter, we call these aspirational gravity (or AG) models. To provide a framework for discussion, let us consider a generic form of the AG models,

where, \(\:{\stackrel{\sim}{M}}\_{t}\) is the actual migration rate (the proportion of the population in a given country at time t-1 who migrated at time t), \(\:\widehat{{M}\_{t-1}}\) is the ex-ante migration intentions (i.e., the proportion of the population who wished to migrate in the previous period), \(\:{D}\_{t-1}\) is a set of push and pull factors typically included in gravity models of migration flows (e.g., GDP per capita, socio-political factors, and environmental conditions in both origin and destination countries), and \(\:{u}\_{t}\) is an error term assumed to be stationary with a constant mean and variance over time.

Despite its simplicity, Eq. ([1](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#Equ1)) still captures the key concept of AG models. Specifically, \(\:{D}\_{t-1}\) corresponds to a set of migration drivers that are typically considered in conventional gravity models, such as economic, environmental, and/or socio-political conditions in both origin and destination countries. \(\:\widehat{{M}\_{t-1}}\) is the new term added to the traditional gravity model, which attempts to capture migration intentions. This valuable addition creates the possibility to infer the link between intentions and flows, which is conceptually important in understanding the long causal chain from migration intention to outcome (Tjaden et al., [2018](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR46 "Tjaden, J., Auer, D., & Laczko, F. (2018). Linking migration intentions with flows: Evidence and potential use. International Migration, 57, 36–57. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1111/imig.12502
                  
                ")). Hereafter, we refer to this link as the migration intention-action transition. Migration intention precedes outcome (Carling & Schewel, [2018](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR15 "Carling, J., & Schewel, K. (2018). Revisiting aspiration and ability in international migration. Journal of Ethnic and Migration Studies, 44, 945–963")). To reflect this temporal process, lagged effects in the measures of migration intention are often introduced in the model.

Empirical estimation of Eq. ([1](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#Equ1)) requires a proper measure of people’s ex-ante intentions to migrate, which is not straightforward because most international migration statistics only count the number of actual, rather than aspiring, migrants. Traditionally, survey data was commonly used to assess migration intentions. The Gallup World Poll (GWP) is the most comprehensive and wide-ranging survey measuring migration intentions (Esipova et al., [2011](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR27 "Esipova, N., Ray, J., & Pugliese, A. (2011). Gallup world poll: The many faces of global migration. United Nations")) by asking individuals, “Ideally, if you had the opportunity, would you like to move permanently to another country, or would you prefer to continue living in this country?”. This survey is conducted annually since 2005 and in over 145 languages. Its overall purpose is to measure people’s attitudes, will, behavior and wellbeing across more than 140 countries, representing 95% of global adults.

However, despite being the trusted source for data on global trends, the GWP data have three key disadvantages. First, accessing these data is very costly, because data collection on a regular basis is expensive and time-consuming. Second, the data are not consistently available over time. For example, data were not collected in some countries during the recent COVID-19 pandemic, thus the time series of migration intentions are incomplete. Finally, the GWP is not suitable for making timely migration forecasts due to the time lag of the data release. As can be seen from Fig. [1](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#Fig1), the survey data are generally released with one or more year(s) delay. When it becomes available at t + 1, the actual migration outcome can already be observed, making the forecast irrelevant.

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1186%2Fs40878-025-00450-2/MediaObjects/40878_2025_450_Fig1_HTML.png)

Nowcasted vs. delayed survey measured migration intention

To make Eq. ([1](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#Equ1)) operable and useful for forecasting, researchers developed innovative approaches to measure migration intentions, most notably the adoption of Google Trends (GT), which collates the spatial-temporal patterns of search queries in a near real-time fashion (Böhme et al., [2020](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR7 "Böhme, M. H., Gröger, A., & Stöhr, T. (2020). Searching for a better life: Predicting international migration with online search keywords. Journal of Development Economics, 142, 102347"); Wanner, [2020](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR48 "Wanner, P. (2020). How well can we estimate immigration trends using Google data? Quality & Quantity, 55, 1181–1202. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s11135-020-01047-w
                  
                "); Avramescu & Wiśniowski, [2021](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR3 "Avramescu, A., & Wiśniowski, A. (2021). Now-casting Romanian migration into the united Kingdom by using Google search engine data. Demographic Research, 45, 1219–1254. 
                  https://doi.org/10.4054/demres.2021.45.40
                  
                "); Carammia et al., [2022](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR12 "Carammia, M., Iacus, S. M., & Wilkin, T. (2022). Forecasting asylum-related migration flows with machine learning and data at scale. Scientific Reports, 12, 1–16")). The rationale for using GT is that online search behavior may reveal people’s ex-ante migration intentions (Varian, [2014](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR47 "Varian, H. R. (2014). Big data: New tricks for econometrics. Journal of Economic Perspectives, 28, 3–28")). There is also evidence that aspiring migrants rely on online information to explore migration opportunities (Maitland & Xu, [2015](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR32 "Maitland, C., & Xu, Y. (2015). A social informatics analysis of refugee mobile phone use. A case study of Za’atari Syrian refugee camp")).

To measure migration intentions, researchers construct country-specific time series based on a set of predefined keywords. The correlations between these time series of keywords and observed migration outcomes are then inferred using Eq. ([1](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#Equ1)), which reveals the possibility for people to realize their desired migration (the intention-action transition). For example, migration-related terms (e.g., “passports,” “visa,” “asylum,” etc.) searched on Google can be proxies for interest in seeking asylum or to migrate. When the volume of these searches increase, it can be an indication of rising intentions to emigrate. And, if these trending intentions correlate with actual flows, the estimated coefficient will be mapped to the forecasting model to predict possible future outcomes.

In general, when adding GT as a direct measure for \(\:\widehat{{M}\_{t-1}}\) to Eq. ([1](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#Equ1)), models tend to better explain and predict migration patterns (Böhme et al., [2020](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR7 "Böhme, M. H., Gröger, A., & Stöhr, T. (2020). Searching for a better life: Predicting international migration with online search keywords. Journal of Development Economics, 142, 102347")). This suggests that AG models are not only valuable for reconciling two competing notions of migration behavior (the utility maximization view and the aspiration-capability paradigm), but also practically better, in terms of forecasting future migration. However, a recent study demonstrated that the explanatory and predictive power of GT is sensitive to model complexity; it tends to explain and predict migration patterns well in simple models, but not as well in complex ones (Qi & Bircan, [2023b](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR38 "Qi, H., & Bircan, T. (2023b). Can Google Trends predict asylum-seekers’ destination choices? EPJ Data Science, 12. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1140/epjds/s13688-023-00419-0
                  
                ")). Specifically, when the model included an auto-regressive component to account for time-varying unobserved factors, the explanatory and predictive power of GT became negligible. This suggests that GT might not necessarily capture migration intentions as intended, but instead, it might capture some unobserved factors that are simultaneously correlated with GT and actual migration flows.

To address the abovementioned empirical irregularities, this article takes a step back to evaluate the validity of GT data. Instead of testing how well GT can predict migration actions, we examine the validity of GT as a measure of people’s ex-ante intentions to migrate. Such a validation is critical both conceptually and empirically. If GT does not capture migration intentions, it will not help us uncover the dynamics of the migration intention-action transition. Empirically, if the link between GT and migration flows is merely based in spurious correlations, the forecasts based on Eq. ([1](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#Equ1)) are unlikely to be robust and reliable. To elaborate on these two points, let us consider two cases. The first case represents all the existing studies discussed earlier which use GT to infer migration behavior (Böhme et al., [2020](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR7 "Böhme, M. H., Gröger, A., & Stöhr, T. (2020). Searching for a better life: Predicting international migration with online search keywords. Journal of Development Economics, 142, 102347"); Wanner, [2020](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR48 "Wanner, P. (2020). How well can we estimate immigration trends using Google data? Quality & Quantity, 55, 1181–1202. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s11135-020-01047-w
                  
                "); Avramescu & Wiśniowski, [2021](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR3 "Avramescu, A., & Wiśniowski, A. (2021). Now-casting Romanian migration into the united Kingdom by using Google search engine data. Demographic Research, 45, 1219–1254. 
                  https://doi.org/10.4054/demres.2021.45.40
                  
                "); Carammia et al., [2022](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#ref-CR12 "Carammia, M., Iacus, S. M., & Wilkin, T. (2022). Forecasting asylum-related migration flows with machine learning and data at scale. Scientific Reports, 12, 1–16")). Specifically, GT search keywords are used as direct measures of migration intentions,

where, \(\:{G}\_{l,t}\) is the trend of \(\:{l}^{th}\) Google search keywords, and \(\:{\beta\:}\_{l}\) is the coefficient governing the relation between migration intentions and \(\:{l}^{th}\) Google search keywords.

The implicit assumption here is that the user population of Google search is representative of the entire population in a country or a geographical area, and, thus, the product of search keywords \(\:{G}\_{l,t}^{{\beta\:}\_{l}}\) in Eq. ([2](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#Equ2)) will reveal the average person’s intention to migrate. However, if this assumption is not satisfied and the selectivity of Google search users is non-random, the AG model in Eq. ([1](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#Equ1)) will produce unreliable forecasts of an average person’s migration actions. To see this, we postulate an alternative case, in which GT is an indirect, rather than a direct, measure of migration intentions. Specifically, we assume that \(\:\widehat{{M}\_{t}}\) is a latent variable which is not only a function of search keywords, but also the adoption rates of Google search engine,

where, \(\:{S}\_{t}\) is the fraction of the population using Google search in a country/geographical area, and \(\:{S}\_{t}\in\:\left(\text{0,1}\right)\). \(\:\gamma\:\) is a selection parameter determining how Google’s user population is selected in terms of their migration intention.

In Eq. ([3](/articles/10.1186/s40878-025-00450-2#Equ3)), if the selection parameter \(\:\gamma\:\) is non-zero, there will be a non-random selection of Google users in terms of mi

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 3: Data on migration and health ---
URL: https://www.migrationdataportal.org/themes/migration-and-health

SUMMARY:
Investments in better migration health data is needed to help reduce the health risks and costs associated with migration, identifying resource priorities, calibrate financing and enhancing public health.Tracking health outcomes relevant to SDGs and disease control programs, and inter and intra country comparisons towards progress towards health goals become impossible without strategies to enable migrant inclusive health and migration management systems at national level. Thelack of reliable data on healthcare for migrants stems from several issues: data is not broken down by migratory status, collection methods are inconsistent, and the data does not accurately reflect diverse migrant populations (refugees, labour migrants, international students, asylum seekers etc).Develop a migration module – a consensus set of core variables to determine migratory status and develop capacities and approaches at country level to enshrine migration modules within existing health data collection systems.

Migration and health
====================

Contents

1.   [Definitions](https://www.migrationdataportal.org/themes/migration-and-health#definitions)
2.   [Data sources](https://www.migrationdataportal.org/themes/migration-and-health#data-sources)
3.   [Way forward in Improving migration health data](https://www.migrationdataportal.org/themes/migration-and-health#way-forward-in-improving-migration-health-data)
    1.   [Further reading](https://www.migrationdataportal.org/themes/migration-and-health#further-reading)

Migration places individuals in situations which may impact their physical and mental well-being. Conditions surrounding the migration process may increase the vulnerability of migrants towards poorer health outcomes, or conversely enable wellbeing and access to healthcare. The impacts on the health of migrants have multiple determinants and may change over time. These impacts may differ across migrant categories and legal status. Migration also cuts across economic and social policies, human rights and equity issues, development agendas, and social norms – all of which are relevant to migration health. The[2030 Sustainable Development Agenda](https://migrationdataportal.org/sdgs#0), the Global Compact of Migrants and Refugees and other international instruments have outlined data at the nexus of migration and health critically important to monitor the Agenda’s progress, including specific progress on the[health-related goal and targets](https://www.migrationdataportal.org/sdgs?node=3)to ensure that “no one is left behind” irrespective of their migration status.

[Back to top](https://www.migrationdataportal.org/themes/migration-and-health#top)
Definitions
-----------

Health is defined as a “state of complete physical, mental and social well-being and not merely the absence of disease or infirmity” ([WHO, 2005](https://apps.who.int/gb/bd/PDF/bd47/EN/constitution-en.pdf?ua=1 "(opens in a new window)")). 

 Health is also a basic human right and an essential component of sustainable development; being and staying healthy is a fundamental precondition for migrants to work, to be productive and to contribute to the social and economic development of their communities of origin and destination.

The concept of migration and health (or migration health) encompasses the idea that there are various factors and conditions that influence the health of migrants. These are referred to as social determinants of health (SDH). A range of socioeconomic, legal, cultural, environmental, and physical environments to individual factors such as lifestyle, age, hereditary, and behavioral factors that impact the health of migrants (Figure 2). Migration itself is considered a [social determinant of health](http://www.who.int/social_determinants/sdh_definition/en/ "(opens in a new window)") because of its potential to impact health outcomes due to factors such as changes in living conditions and access to healthcare. Migrants’ health care access is also to a large extent determined by the availability, accessibility, acceptability, appropriateness, affordability and quality of services in the host community or country. There may be differences in the disease profiles and health risk factors between migrant and host populations, or inequalities in the access/uptake of preventive interventions and in treatment outcomes.

[![Image 2: Migration and the social determinants of health](https://www.migrationdataportal.org/sites/g/files/tmzbdl251/files/styles/embedded_845/public/2018-05/0503_IOM_8IG_Health%20LG-04-04.png?itok=OFqFec8O)](https://www.migrationdataportal.org/sites/g/files/tmzbdl251/files/2018-05/0503_IOM_8IG_Health%20LG-04-04.png "<a href=\"https://www.migrationdataportal.org/sites/g/files/tmzbdl251/files/2018-05/0503_IOM_8IG_Health%20LG-04-04.png\" download>Download</a>")

The relationship between migration and health is complex. and its impact varies considerably across migrant groups, from person to person within such groups, and during the phases of migration (Figure 2). Migrants are affected by social inequalities and are exposed to several experiences during the migration process which put their physical, mental and social well-being at risk. Conditions surrounding the migration process may exacerbate health vulnerabilities and risk behaviors. Conversely, it can be an enabler for achieving better health trajectories, such as the case of a newly arrived refugee as part of a humanitarian settlement programme getting access to treatment for a chronic disease. Due to their irregular status, stigma, discrimination, language, cultural barriers and low-income levels, irregular migrants may be excluded from accessing primary health care services, vaccination and health-promotion interventions.

A growing body of research evidence points to varying gradients of benefits and risk factors for the health of migrants (and their families) through the stages of migration ([BMJ, 2024](https://www.bmj.com/migrant-health "(opens in a new window)"); [WHO, 2022](https://www.who.int/publications-detail-redirect/9789240054462 "(opens in a new window)"); [PLOS, 2020](https://collections.plos.org/collection/refugee-health-special-issue/ "(opens in a new window)")) (Figure 2). The process of migration includes different phases (pre-departure, travel and transit, destination and integration, and return), where the health of migrants can be adversely affected or be positively enabled. The interplay and impacts of migration on the health of migrants themselves. Human mobility may also impact on public health with respect to the prevention and control of emerging infectious diseases. For instance, moving from a country with high malaria endemicity to one which is on verge of elimination.

[![Image 3: Factors influencing health of migrants along migration phases](https://www.migrationdataportal.org/sites/g/files/tmzbdl251/files/styles/embedded_845/public/2018-05/Health%20and%20migration%20phases_0.png?itok=N9WWwb7A)](https://www.migrationdataportal.org/sites/g/files/tmzbdl251/files/2018-05/Health%20and%20migration%20phases_0.png "<a href=\"https://www.migrationdataportal.org/sites/g/files/tmzbdl251/files/2018-05/Health%20and%20migration%20phases_0.png\" download>Download</a>")

Some countries/territories enforce mandatory detention for many irregular migrants where h ealth-related vulnerabilities may exacerbate due to lack of access to health services, nutrition, inadequate hygiene and sanitation within densely populated living spaces, and violence. The length of detention has been associated with the severity of mental disorders and psychosocial issues.

Many international migrant workers are employed in precarious work settings, within dangerous, difficult and demeaning(3Ds) jobs ([ILO, n.d.](https://www.ilo.org/safework/areasofwork/hazardous-work/lang--en/index.htm "(opens in a new window)")),with low wages, hazardous working conditions, with limited social protection and occupational health rights. Migrants’ access to health services has increasingly been a key indicator of people-centered, rights-based, inclusive and equitable health systems that aim at reducing health inequities, but the social exclusion of vulnerable migrant groups continues to be common in the absence of explicit affirmative policies.

[Back to top](https://www.migrationdataportal.org/themes/migration-and-health#top)
Data sources
------------

Migration health data may be broadly defined as data relevant to characterizing the health and social determinants of various migrant populations (e.g. the non-communicable disease burden in international labor migrants); data relating to extent of access to health care system by migrant typology; to data to better understand disease spread across geospatial areas along human mobility pathways. Data can be both quantitative, such as epidemiological profiles on refugee populations; and qualitative, describing risk and resiliency factors.

Despite the clarion calls for better migration data to inform public health policy and practice, migrants are largely invisible in official data due to lack of integration of migration variables within routine National/Sub-National Health Information Systems (HIMS)([Vidal, E. M., & Wickramage, K. P., 2024](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38313145/ "(opens in a new window)")). Routine data sources range from population health surveys, (vertical) disease control programs, hospital registries to health insurance schemes (Table 1). The lack of inclusion of migrant variables within HIMS makes disaggregation of health outcomes by migrant status challenging– making it hard to track progress at national/global levels, leading to opinion based, rather than evidence-based decision making.

Migration health data exists not only within health sector but may also be collected through operations/services rendered by other sectors such as Immigration and border management, refugee resettlement services, Foreign Employment Bureau, Health Insurance providers and through harnessing big data (Figure 3). Such sources of MH data often remain unmapped and unlinked to health authorities and HIMS. For instance, analysis of health insurance claims/payments made within international labour migrant health insurance schemes are housed within Foreign Employment authorities and can provide critical data on morbidities, injuries and deaths of outbound migrant workers, who often work precarious employment settings ([IOM, 2021](https://migrationhealthresearch.iom.int/sites/g/files/tmzbdl256/files/publications/Report%20on%20Scoping%20Review_Final%20Copy.pdf "(opens in a new window)")). There is a need to make better use of existing data captured through the health and migration management ecosystems. Methods such as data linkage may be used to ‘link’ disparate data sets and provide insights on migrant health outcomes, especially across the migration trajectory ([Burns et al., 2019](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6381442/ "(opens in a new window)"); [Bozorgmehr et al., 2023](https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lanepe/article/PIIS2666-7762(23)00163-1/fulltext "(opens in a new window)")).

The utilization of innovative ‘big data’ signifies a significant opportunity to harness migration health data in conjunction with sophisticated analytical techniques like machine learning. By harnessing diverse data sources such as mobile phone Call Data Records (CDR), satellites, and social media, and integrating/superimposing data from population mobility mapping methods ([IOM DTM](https://dtm.iom.int/ "(opens in a new window)"), [HBMM](https://publications.iom.int/books/health-border-and-mobility-management-framework-framework-empower-governments-and-communities "(opens in a new window)")) including community-based approaches ([IOM PMM](https://www.iom.int/sites/g/files/tmzbdl486/files/documents/IOM-Migration-Health-Population-Mobility-Mapping-Infosheet.pdf "(opens in a new window)")), data relevant to human mobility and health can be generated. In particular, the integration of CDRs acquired from telecommunications and social media platforms with relevant Geographic Information System (GIS) data, alongside a broad array of datasets encompassing epidemiological maps, climatological maps, precipitation maps, and vectorial density data, flight and travel information, has emerged as a potent approach for comprehending the dynamics of infectious disease transmission ([Li et al., 2023](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC10247678/ "(opens in a new window)"), [Perrotta et al., 2022](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9299334/ "(opens in a new window)")). This multifaceted strategy enables a better understanding of disease spread patterns and facilitates the development of targeted interventions.

**Examples of Routine data sources at the national/sub-national level:**

**A) Civil registration, vital statistics and population censuses:**

Administrative data sources and censuses provide information on the births and deaths (and cause of deaths) of people. If information on a person’s length of stay/date of entry into a country, citizenship status and country of birth are captured, such data may identify whether someone is a migrant, and be used to analyze health outcomes based on these variables. However only very few countries capture variables by migratory status ([Bozorgmehr et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666776223001631 "(opens in a new window)")).

In **Denmark**, a comprehensive Civil Registration System ([CRS](https://ugeskriftet.dk/dmj/danish-civil-registration-system "(opens in a new window)")) assigns a unique 10-digit Civil Personal Register number to all individuals, including refugees and specific migrant groups. This system records vital data such as country of birth, citizenship, date of immigration, and parental nationality, aiding in the identification of refugees and migrants. Additionally, the personal identifier is interconnected across various national registers, including health, education, and labor. By integrating these systems, health information can be efficiently linked with migration indicators, reducing the burden on healthcare personnel by eliminating the need for independent data collection. The CPR-number can also be utilized within data-linkage as an important research tool in epidemiological research e.g., as regards cancer and psychiatric epidemiology. It is possible to investigate the association between disease or death in individuals and place of birth and residence, immigration and emigration status and environmental exposures at place of residence.

[![Image 4: Migration Health Data Sources (Mapping)](https://www.migrationdataportal.org/sites/g/files/tmzbdl251/files/styles/embedded_845/public/2024-04/Health%20IG_April%202024%20%281%29.png?itok=FLK6LYxJ)](https://www.migrationdataportal.org/sites/g/files/tmzbdl251/files/2024-04/Health%20IG_April%202024%20%281%29.png "Migration Health Data Sources (Mapping)")

**B) Household surveys:**

**[Demographic and Health Surveys](https://dhsprogram.com/ "(opens in a new window)")(DHS)** are nationally representative household surveys that provide data on a wide range of health- and nutrition-related information. The program has collected data through more than 400 surveys in over 90 countries. The high-income countries that are often the destination for international migrants normally do not conduct household surveys. Respondents to DHS are usually female head of households, and such surveys are tailored to the needs of a particular country while containing several basic components that are comparable across all countries. DHS surveys are not designed to specifically target international migrants in the sampling design. In the DHS-VII, international migrants are those who choose "abroad" in response to the variable "region of previous residence". To identify non-migrants the DHS uses "years lived in place of residence".While there are clear limitations to this migrant variables/identifier, DHS data can beused toanalyze health outcomes collected in survey. A 2012[review](https://academic.oup.com/ije/article/41/6/1602/747345 "(opens in a new window)")of 85 DHS national surveys found that a dedicated module with detailed information on migration was only be present in 12 surveys. For children, their migration status can sometimes be identified through their mothers' migration status. In cases where a separate section on international migration appears in the questionnaire, children who left the household to go abroad can also be identified, along with their basic characteristics.

**[Multiple Indicator Cluster Survey (MICS)](https://mics.unicef.org/ "(opens in a new window)")**is a household survey programme developed by UNICEF in the mid 90's to assist countries in filling data gaps for monitoring the situation of children and women. It is capable of producing statistically sound, internationally comparable estimates of these indicators. In the 6th iteration of the survey (MICS6), two types of migratory status are featured: international migrant and non-migrant (which includes internal migrants). For MICS, international migrants refer to those who choose "overseas/outside of country" in response to the variable "place of living prior to moving to current place" and "province prior to moving to current place". The survey also uses different variables from DHS to identify non-migrants. The MICS uses "duration of living in current place". In the MICS, respondents who answer "always/since birth" are included as non-migrants.

_**Findings from household surveys:**_

The immunization of children is paramount for maintaining public health and community well-being. Measles, one of the most highly contagious viruses to humans, serves as a barometer for both inadequate vaccination coverage and limited access to essential healthcare services, particularly among vulnerable populations. Analysis of survey data available only from 8 out of 15 countries published by WHO reveals lower measles vaccination rates among international migrant children compared to those in the host population, highlighting disparities in healthcare accessibility and the need for targeted interventions (Table 1).

**Table 1: Children who received Measles vaccination, by migrant status (percentage)**

Country Survey/ Round*Year International Migrant Host population
Armenia DHS-VII 2015-2016 27.3%60.5%
Benin DHS-VII 2017-2018 45.0%47.5%
Burundi DHS-VII 2016-2017 63.0%69.3%
Cameroon DHS-VII 2018 32.7%49.9%
Central African Republic MICS6 2018-2019 55.6%54.2%
Ethiopia DHS-VII 2016 39.7%41.3%
Gambia MICS6 2018 86.7%85.0%

**[Household Survey Databank](https://repositorio.cepal.org/handle/11362/31828 "(opens in a new window)") (Banco de Datos de Encuestas de Hogares; BADEHOG):** is a repository of The United Nations Economic Commission for Latin America and the Caribbean (ECLAC). It is made up of a set of household surveys conducted by the countries of Latin America and the Caribbean since the 1990s. These surveys are conducted by the National Statistics Offices or other public agencies of the respective countries and share the characteristic of being the source of information officially used to measure poverty, inequality and various social indicators. ![Empirische Politik UDE Logo](https://i0.wp.com/www.politik-wissenschaft.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/04/Logo-bearbeitet-e1709722858416.png?fit=109%2C95&ssl=1)

# A Ph.D. Student’s Approach to Data Availability Challenges

## Beitrags-Navigation

![](https://www.politik-wissenschaft.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/pexels-kevin-ku-92347-577585-scaled.jpg)

By [Adriana Cassis](https://adrianacassis.com)

As is the case in many other areas of research, scholars of immigrant political participation face challenges of data availability and accessibility. This problem affects the type of questions that can be researched and the methods that can be applied. One solution is to collect one’s own data, but for doctoral students like myself, a more plausible answer to getting around this problem is to use existing data in innovative ways. In this blog entry, I will describe how I merged existing data sets to solve my data needs.

Initiatives such as the Ethnic and Migrant Minorities (EMM) Survey Registry demonstrate the deliberate efforts of scholars in the field of migration studies to make data available and accessible. The thousands of surveys collected in the survey registry cover a wide range of topics and methodologies. Many are EU-funded projects with multiple survey waves in several countries while others are nationally funded and have a subnational scope. This demonstrates that the problem is not an absence of data on the immigrant population.

The challenges are the availability and scope of the data. When data is available, there are issues regarding the themes and populations covered, which hinders the ability to make comparisons across locations, time periods, and topics. For example, if you are interested in the political behavior and participation of immigrants, you will quickly find that many of the surveys that cover this topic are national in scope. Consequently, to compare across countries you may need to rely on multiple surveys from different timepoints and with varying methodology. Or, if there is a longitudinal multi-country survey, chances are that some key measurements are missing.

Faced with such problems, I decided that my best bet was to take different existing surveys and essentially merge them into a new dataset. That way I could pick and choose the data that best fit my needs. However, this was much easier said than done, since I first had to find surveys that had data I was interested in, make sure that it was actually available in full, and analyze whether the surveys were similar enough to merge.

For my research purposes, I needed data on the transnational political participation of Turkish immigrants that explicitly controlled for immigrant-based characteristics. I decided to use the Immigrant German Election Study I (IMGES I) and the Dutch Ethnic Minority Election Study (DEMES). This decision came after a review of countless codebooks, which were my main source of information and through which I assessed how similar the surveys were, both methodologically and substantively. Both DEMES and IMGES sampled the population I wanted to study, took place around the same time period, and were comparable in terms of my main research topics.

After finding the surveys came the most difficult task: wrangling the data. This requires the researcher to become fluent in two separate datasets in order to merge them into a new dataset. In practice, this means relabeling and recoding variables so that they make sense in both content and form. For example, to construct a variable of self-identification, I had to choose from several questions in the DEMES questionnaire that asked respondents about their connection to the Netherlands, their pride in being Dutch, their sense of belonging to their country of origin, or their view of themselves as citizens. In the IMGES questionnaire, on the other hand, I could only rely on one question about how respondents would describe themselves, which again had several possible answers. After selecting the appropriate IMGES and DEMES self-identity variables and relabeling them, I had to recode them so that they were on the same scale. This process was repeated for all the variables of interest.

Although merging the data sets is a cost-effective solution, it is still a time-consuming process that has inherent limitations. One limitation of this approach is that some topics cannot be researched because they are not measured in both surveys. Another limitation is the appropriateness of merging variables that collect information from differently worded questions. For instance, the question of whether one has a strong sense of belonging to a country is distinct from the question as to whether one would describe oneself as a national of that country. This raises questions about the validity of assuming that they are measuring the same concept. Lastly, there is the issue of proper weighting after merging datasets, which must be addressed on a case-by-case basis.

While merging data sets is neither a foolproof nor a revolutionary method, it provides a solution to the problem of data availability. Furthermore, it is a practical solution for times when gathering one’s own data is not possible, yet the research questions require data that cannot be found in one single dataset. It is indeed this technique that makes the analys of patterns of transnational political participation, with data that has never before been used together, possible.

References

Goerres, Achim, Spies, Dennis C., & Mayer, Sabrina (2023). Immigrant German Election Study (IMGES). *GESIS, Cologne. ZA7495 Data file Version 2.0.0,*[*https://doi.org/10.4232/1.14187*](https://doi.org/10.4232/1.14187)

Morales, Laura, Saji, Ami, Méndez, Mónica, Bergh, Johannes, & Bernat, Anikó. (2020, May 25). The EMM (Ethnic and Migrant Minorities) Survey Registry. Zenodo. <http://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.3841433>

M. Lubbers; T. Sipma; N. Spierings (2021): DUTCH ETHNIC MINORITY ELECTION STUDY 2021 (DEMES 2021). V2: DANS Data Station Social Sciences and Humanities.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Data on migration is often incomplete or inaccessible, limiting the ability to generate comprehensive insights and reliable trends. This results in significant gaps in understanding migration patterns.
Quality and completeness of migration data vary significantly across regions, leading to inconsistent findings and challenges in making cross-regional comparisons in migration research.
Informal migration is difficult to quantify due to lack of official records and varied definitions, complicating efforts to understand its impact on urbanization and migration trends.
